day.added: integer interning (-5 to 256), identity operators (is, is not), membership operators (in, not in)

system_deviation.summary: Practice volume restructuring based on cognitive science research. Reduces Block 3 problem count, adds interleaving, micro-rests, self-explanation, spaced retrieval, and reduces Review Day intensity.

changes.change: Block 3 problem count: min=10/max=25 → min=8/max=12 (standard days), min=6/max=10 (Review Days). Structure template is binding; curriculum spec is advisory when it exceeds the cap.
Interleaving requirement added to Block 3 tier structure. Problems must mix sub-topics within tiers.
Micro-rest convention: single note at Block 3 top ("10–30 second breather between problems").
Self-explanation prompts added to Tier 2/3 problem template (element 7 in per_problem spec).
Block 5 expanded from 1h to 1.5–2h. New task #1: spaced retrieval of 2–3 problems from Day N-2/N-3.
Block 3 time allocation reduced from 3h to 2–2.5h. Freed time reallocated to Block 5.
Review Day quiz: 30 → 20 questions, with 3–5 recycled from prior week for spacing.
Review Day problems: 12–15 → 6–10, untimed, with cross-week spacing.
Review Day structured time: ~10h → ~6.5–7.5h. Remaining time unstructured.
Objective coverage distribution guidance added: coverage must be distributed across Block 2 micro-exercises, Block 3 problems, and Block 4 project stages.
Deep work ceiling principle added to rules.xml: Block 3 + Block 4 ≤ 4h/day (Ericsson 1993).
Consolidation-over-volume principle added to rules.xml.

system_deviation.rationale: Ericsson (1993): deliberate practice ceiling ~4h/day. Rohrer & Taylor (2006): 9 problems = identical retention to 27. Rohrer & Taylor (2007): interleaving tripled delayed scores. Taylor & Rohrer (2010): interleaving doubled next-day scores. Bönstrup et al. (2019): skill gains occur during rest periods. Feld, Weis & Born (2016): sleep consolidation has limited capacity. Sweller: cognitive load theory — working memory constraints. Wilson et al. (2019): optimal error rate ~15.87% (85% rule). Full research report: 2026-03-11.

system_deviation.scope: review-day packages (confirmed on Day 07; expected to generalize)
system_deviation.description: Two extended gates produce expected false-positive warnings on review-day packages. These are structural mismatches between standard-day gate logic and review-day format — not content problems.
system_deviation.gate: pytest-codeblocks executes # YOUR CODE HERE stubs and fails on AssertionError or NoneType. Stubs are the correct deliverable on review days. Warn is expected and non-blocking on review-day packages.

Objective distribution check fires for all objectives "concentrated in block2". Review days route objective references through Block 2 (Spaced Retrieval) by design — there is no Block 4 to distribute to. Warn is expected and non-blocking on review-day packages.
system_deviation.action: Treat these warns as expected during currently verified review-day verification runs unless future review-day evidence contradicts this. Do not investigate as content defects.

system_deviation.summary: Block 2 + Block 4 combined problem count range (8-15) exceeds Phase 1's single-block 8-12 cap (SD-001). Phase 2 has two problem-producing blocks (DSA Implementation + Platform Practice) vs Phase 1's single Block 3. Retention must be monitored.

changes.change: Block 2 (DSA Implementation): tiered, 6-10 problems (T1 warm-up 1-2, T2 core 3-5, T3 advanced 2-3). Interleaving rule carries forward from SD-001.
Block 4 (Platform Practice): untiered, 2-5 problems from LeetCode / StrataScratch / DataLemur.
Combined standard-day range: 8-15 problems. Day 27 (16 per curriculum — timed-mixed outlier) caps at 15 in the generated package with explicit note.

system_deviation.rationale:
Phase 2 is the first phase with two distinct problem tracks (algorithmic DSA + applied data science via platforms). Rohrer & Taylor (2006) single-domain 9-vs-27 retention finding was within-skill; cross-track parallelization isn't addressed. Ericsson (1993) 4h deliberate-practice ceiling applies per-skill — Phase 2 has 3 skills (DSA, Stats, Platform), each within 4h individually.
Monitor: Week 5 review-day (Day 35) timed-practice performance vs Day 21 baseline. If retention degrades significantly, revise to Block 2 cap 6-8 / Block 4 cap 2-3 / combined 8-11.
system_deviation.affected_files: structure_phase2.xml (Block 2 problem_count spec, Block 4 problem_count spec)

system_deviation.summary: Phase 2 curriculum specifies 4-block 11h review days (per python_bootcamp_curriculum.md Weeks 4-6). Structure template applies SD-001 reduced-intensity principles to compress to 3-block ~6.5-7.5h structured + unstructured remainder, matching Phase 1 review-day pattern.

changes.change: Hybrid quiz: curriculum 35 questions (20 DSA + 15 Stats) → structure 24 questions (14 DSA + 10 Stats). Scaled from SD-001's 30→20 reduction.
Mixed practice: curriculum 10 TIMED problems → structure 6-10 UNTIMED problems. Removes time pressure, adds cross-week spacing (matches SD-001 Phase 1 review).
Integration project: curriculum 4h → structure 3h. Same reduction as SD-001 Phase 1 review.
Evening Reflection: curriculum 1h formal block → structure UNSTRUCTURED remainder. No formal R4 block.
Total structured: curriculum 11h → structure ~6.5-7.5h. Block structure R1 (hybrid quiz + gaps ~2.5h) + R2 (spaced retrieval practice, 1.5-2h, untimed) + R3 (integrative project, ~3h).

system_deviation.rationale:
SD-001's research basis (Rohrer & Taylor 2006, Ericsson 1993, Bönstrup et al. 2019, Feld et al. 2016) is phase-independent. Applying the curriculum's 11h review day would waste the deliberate-practice consolidation gains without evidence of benefit, and taxes a solo learner's sustained energy budget.
T1 co-design decision 4B (2026-04-17): prioritize SD-001 research discipline over brief literalism. Phase 2's hybrid DSA+Stats content is accommodated by the 35q→24q scaling and the 10-timed→6-10-untimed reduction, both preserving cross- domain integration with reduced intensity.
If Phase 2 timed-interview-discipline training is desired in the future (e.g., pre-interview prep sprints), that's an additive practice mode, not a review-day replacement.
system_deviation.affected_files: structure_phase2.xml (review_day_variant block structure, review_day_variant section_overrides quiz count)

system_deviation.description: Phase 2 standard days have Stats objectives (typically 4-5 of 10) structurally concentrated in Block 3 (Stats Midday), because Block 2 is DSA-only and Block 4 is Platform-only (primarily DSA). Gate 5 (Completeness / objective distribution) will warn "Stats objectives concentrated in block3" on standard-day packages. Pattern parallels SD-002's review-day warning.
system_deviation.gate: Objective distribution check fires for Stats-category objectives concentrated in Block 3. This is structural, not a content defect — Block 2 is the DSA track (DSA objectives), Block 4 is the platform track (primarily DSA reinforcement). Stats objectives have no cross-block distribution path on standard days. Warn is expected and non-blocking on Phase 2 standard-day packages.
system_deviation.action: Treat as expected during Phase 2 standard-day verification runs unless future evidence contradicts. Do not investigate as content defect. A future Gate 5 enhancement could accept per-track concentration; that enhancement is out of T1 transition scope.

system_deviation.scope: Phase 2 standard-day packages (Day 23 onwards) that embed hardcoded LeetCode problem URLs in the Resource Library or Block 4 platform-practice section
system_deviation.description:
Day 23 operator authorization permits hardcoded markdown links to LeetCode problem pages (https://leetcode.com/problems/<slug>/) in place of the Gate-8-sanctioned [Search: "title site:leetcode.com"] pattern. Rationale: LeetCode problem pages are publicly viewable in a browser; the HTTP 403 returned to automated WebFetch calls is a bot-gating response, not a signal that the page is missing or login-walled. Problem slugs are stable across LC's history (verified by operator), so hardcoded links survive page revisions and continue to resolve for the learner. Hardcoding provides a direct click-through instead of forcing the learner through a search step.
Gate 8 (Platform Attribution) will FAIL with "Bare URL to LeetCode without [Search: ...] format" on every line containing a LC markdown link. This is a structural mismatch between Gate 8's strict anti-fabrication rule and the operator's authorized hardcoding policy — not a content defect. Gate 8's original intent was to prevent fabricated platform titles and unverified URLs; operator-verified stable slugs are categorically different.
system_deviation.gate: Gate 8 fires "Bare URL to LeetCode without [Search: ...] format" once per line containing a LC URL. On Day 23 this produces 12 FAIL entries (6 Block 2 Resource Library rows + 3 Block 4 Resource Library rows + 3 Block 4 problem detail lines). All 12 FAILs are authorized. They are NOT blocking delivery under operator override.
system_deviation.rationale:
Gate 8's rule was written against the Phase 1 threat model (fabricated LC titles, unverified URLs). It predates the Phase 2 operator protocol that treats operator-verified stable slugs as a trusted embedding pathway. Reconciling Gate 8 logic with the authorized hardcoding policy is a pending verifier enhancement; until that lands, SD-006 classifies the 12 FAILs as structural/authorized, not as content defects.
Gate 9 (lychee HTTP validation) continues to validate that each hardcoded URL resolves at verification time — a 404 from lychee would surface as a real content defect and trigger operator review. As of Day 23, all 9 LC URLs pass lychee.
Acceptance checklists citing SD-006 on Gate 8 FAIL preserve GREEN/YELLOW trust classification; without SD-006, the default protocol reads a required-gate FAIL as RED.
system_deviation.action: When evaluating a Phase 2 package's delivery readiness: (a) count Gate 8 FAILs whose message matches "Bare URL to LeetCode without [Search: ...] format" and verify each FAIL line contains a legitimate leetcode.com/problems/<slug>/ hardcoded URL; (b) subtract those from the required-gate FAIL count; (c) require Gate 9 lychee to PASS (every hardcoded URL must resolve); (d) proceed with acceptance if the residual required-gate FAIL count is zero. Do NOT treat the Gate 8 FAILs themselves as blocking.
system_deviation.affected_files: packages/day23_two_pointers_variance.md (and subsequent Phase 2 packages that follow the same hardcoding pattern), scripts/verify_package.py (Gate 8 logic unchanged pending verifier enhancement)

item.reason: Requires for/if/def not taught on Day 2
item.placement_note: Block 4 Type C — string functions built as documented, type-hinted functions with builtin comparison

item.reason: Requires def/for/while/if not taught on Day 2
item.placement_note: Block 4 Stage 3 — built from scratch using functions, integrated with DC-001 string functions
Simplified on Day 3 (no def, loop-based only)

phase_scope.category: int, float, str, bool, None, NoneType, type(), type conversion (int(), float(), str(), bool()), integer interning (-5 to 256)

arithmetic (+, -, *, /, //, %, **), comparison (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), logical (and, or, not), identity (is, is not), membership (in, not in), assignment (=, +=, -=, *=, /=), ternary (x if cond else y), matrix multiplication (@), unpacking (*args, **kwargs, a, *b, c)

.upper(), .lower(), .strip(), .lstrip(), .rstrip(), .replace(), .find(), .count(), .startswith(), .endswith(), .split(), .join(), .title(), .capitalize(), .swapcase(), .center(), .ljust(), .rjust(), .zfill(), .isdigit(), .isalpha(), .isalnum(), .isspace(), .isupper(), .islower(), f-strings, .format(), %-formatting, raw strings (r""), escape characters (\n, \t, \\), string indexing (s[i]), slicing (s[start:stop:step], negative), len(), concatenation (+), repetition (*), immutability

if / elif / else (nested), for loops, while loops, break, continue, pass, range(stop), range(start, stop), range(start, stop, step), enumerate(), zip(), nested loops, matrix traversal, multiplication table, loop patterns (accumulator, counter, search, filter)

creation, indexing, slicing, mutability, aliasing, shallow vs deep copy, nested lists (2D matrices), .append(), .extend(), .insert(), .remove(), .pop(), .index(), .count(), .sort(), sorted(), .reverse(), reversed(), .copy(), list performance (.append() O(1) amortized, .insert(0, x) O(n)), list internals (dynamic arrays)
creation, immutability, packing/unpacking, tuple as dict keys, namedtuple (preview)

creation (literal, dict(), comprehension), accessing ([], .get()), adding/updating, deleting (del, .pop()), .keys(), .values(), .items(), .update(), .setdefault(), dict patterns (frequency counting, grouping, lookup tables, inversion)
creation, set operations (union |, intersection &, difference -, symmetric difference ^), .add(), .remove(), .discard(), frozenset, hash()
Counter, defaultdict, OrderedDict, deque, (imports: from collections import X, import collections)

def, return, parameters (positional, keyword, default), *args, **kwargs, LEGB scope rule, global keyword (discouraged), closures, currying, lambda, higher-order: map(), filter(), functools.reduce(), docstrings (Google / NumPy style), basic type hints, decorators (timing decorator, retry decorator — preview), memoization (manual dict, @lru_cache), self-written my_map() / my_filter() / my_reduce()

list comprehensions (basic, conditional, nested), dict comprehensions, set comprehensions, generator expressions (lazy evaluation), yield, custom generators, infinite generators, iterator protocol (__iter__, __next__, iter(), next(), StopIteration), sys.getsizeof, tracemalloc (preview)

open(), modes (r, w, a, r+, b, t), .read(), .readline(), .readlines(), .write(), .writelines(), with statement (context manager), custom context managers (__enter__, __exit__), csv.reader, csv.writer, csv.DictReader, csv.DictWriter, json.load, json.dump, json.loads, json.dumps, pathlib.Path (preferred over os.path), Path.rglob(), sys.argv for basic CLI

try / except / else / finally, catching specific exceptions, raise, re-raise, custom exception classes (inheriting from Exception), EAFP vs LBYL (Python philosophy), assert, logging module: logging.debug/info/warning/error/critical, logging.basicConfig, exception hierarchy (BaseException -> Exception -> specific)

os, sys, datetime (date, datetime, timedelta), collections, itertools (combinations, permutations), functools (reduce, lru_cache), re (regex basics), math (+ from math import sqrt), random, copy (+ from copy import deepcopy), typing (Any, Dict, List, Optional, Tuple, Union), abc (ABC, abstractmethod), dataclasses (dataclass, field)

base/derived class, super(), method overriding, multiple inheritance, MRO (__mro__), diamond problem, polymorphism via duck typing, ABC, @abstractmethod, composition vs inheritance ("has-a" vs "is-a"), SOLID principles overview (Single Responsibility, Open-Closed), LinkedListNode class design (preview only), TreeNode class design (preview only)

comparison: __eq__, __lt__, __le__, __gt__, __ge__, arithmetic: __add__, __sub__, __mul__, __truediv__, container: __len__, __getitem__, __setitem__, __contains__, __iter__, context: __enter__, __exit__

base case + recursive case, call stack visualization, 3 laws of recursion, simple recursion (factorial, power, fibonacci, sum of list, string reversal, count digits, palindrome check, GCD Euclidean), Tower of Hanoi, recursive binary search, recursion vs iteration trade-offs, stack overflow, sys.setrecursionlimit (tail recursion is NOT optimized in Python), divide and conquer (merge sort from scratch), backtracking (subsets, permutations, simplified N-Queens, letter combinations of a phone number), memoization (manual dict, @lru_cache formal usage), recursive data structure traversal (nested lists, nested dicts, filesystem via pathlib)

import numpy as np (and canonical import numpy), np.array, np.zeros, np.ones, np.arange, np.linspace, np.random.default_rng, np.random.* (general), np.eye, np.insert, np.cumsum, np.allclose, np.sqrt, np.sum, np.dot, @ operator, np.linalg.inv, np.linalg.det, array properties (.shape, .dtype, .ndim, .size), reshaping (.reshape, .ravel, .flatten), indexing (basic, boolean, fancy), element-wise operations, broadcasting rules, aggregations along axes (.sum, .mean, .std, .min, .max, axis=0/1), linear algebra basics (matrix add/mult, transpose, determinant 2x2/3x3)

import pandas as pd (and canonical import pandas), pd.Series, pd.DataFrame (from dict, list of dicts, CSV), pd.read_csv, pd.merge, pd.concat, pd.to_datetime, pd.json_normalize, pandas.io.json.json_normalize (legacy path, also in scope), indexing: [], .loc[], .iloc[], .query(), inspection: .head, .tail, .info, .describe, .shape, .dtypes, .value_counts, .unique, .nunique, filtering: boolean masks, multiple conditions with & and |, sorting: .sort_values, .sort_index, missing data: .isna, .fillna, .dropna, interpolation (basic), groupby: .groupby, .agg, .transform, string accessor: .str.contains, .str.extract, .str.replace, datetime accessor: .dt, apply: .apply (row/col), .applymap, reshaping: .pivot_table, .melt, .stack, .unstack, .join, window: .rolling, .expanding, .shift, .rank, basic matplotlib.pyplot (plot, figure, savefig, show, title, xlabel, ylabel)

recursive flattening (dict, list, mixed), iterative flattening with a stack (preview), pd.json_normalize, pandas.io.json.json_normalize, unflattening, leaf extraction, path-to-value search, dot-notation key joining (a.b.c)

Day 1: integer interning (-5 to 256), identity operators (is, is not), membership operators (in, not in) — ADDED per v4 package. Day 2: .isalpha, .isalnum, from-scratch string method coding — ADDED. DC-001: 10 from-scratch string implementations — placed on Day 6. DC-002: Text Analyzer mini-project — placed on Day 6. DC-003: Number Guessing Game — simplified and placed on Day 3 (no def, loop-based only). SD-001 scope impact: practice volume restructuring affected structure/quality gates, not concept scope. SD-002 scope impact: review-day expected warnings (Gate 5 + 10), not concept scope.

The following are LATER-PHASE constructs that did NOT appear in Phase 1 exercises/problems/projects (only in preview notes): linked lists (Phase 3 Week 7), trees / BSTs / tries (Phase 3), graphs / BFS / DFS (Phase 4), dynamic programming as technique (Phase 4), heaps as structure (Phase 2 Week 6 via heapq), regression (Phase 4), scipy / scipy.stats (Phase 2 Week 5+), statsmodels (Phase 2 Week 6), sklearn and beyond (Phase 8+), bisect (Phase 2 Week 5), heapq (Phase 2 Week 6), async/await, sockets, network I/O — not in curriculum.
